# Always-Visible UI Pattern

A recipe for HUDs, leaderboards, kill feeds, ammo counters, and any UI that updates from `onGameTick`. Drops per-frame UI cost to near zero on Chromebooks and other low-end clients.

## Why this matters

Jamango games run in the browser across a wide range of hardware, from low end Chromebooks and phones up to high end PCs. Every game you ship needs to be playable on the slowest device a player owns. The total frame budget on those devices is around 16 ms (60 FPS) or 33 ms (30 FPS), shared between physics, rendering, network, and your scripts. There is no room to waste.

UI is one of the largest single consumers of that budget when written naively. A HUD that rebuilds `innerHTML` every tick burns milliseconds on HTML parsing and layout. A leaderboard with per cell `style.X` writes across ten rows can invalidate fifty style records per frame for no visual change. A `filter: drop-shadow` on a handful of elements can cost several milliseconds per frame on integrated graphics. Stacked together, these turn a 60 FPS game into a 20 FPS one before you have written a single line of gameplay.

This pattern keeps per frame UI cost effectively zero. A tick with nothing to change touches the DOM zero times. A tick with one value change touches the DOM once. Animations run on the compositor without involving JavaScript at all. Treat it as the baseline for any always visible UI, not an optimization to add later.

## When to use this

Use it for any UI that:
- Is visible every frame (HUDs, leaderboards, kill feeds, ammo counters, score bars, status indicators)
- Updates from `onGameTick` based on player state, networked traits, or engine state
- Has scale, fade, or pulse animations

Skip it for:
- One shot modals that mount on key press and disappear (just build on open, tear down on close)
- UI driven only by user input (event handlers do the work, no tick involvement)
- World positioned labels following a moving entity (still apply the pattern, but the `style.left` / `style.top` writes are legitimately per frame)

## Where to put it

Jamango reserves fixed corner regions for its built in UI in both edit and play mode. If your HUD positions itself in those corners, it will overlap the engine controls and become unreadable or unclickable.

Reserved by the engine, do not place HUD here:
- **Top left corner**: TAB key indicator, chat input toggle, mode toggles.
- **Top right corner**: home, lock, currency toggle, fullscreen, plus editor toggles in edit mode.
- **Right side (whole vertical strip)**: on mobile this is the camera look area, touch and drag here rotates the view. UI placed on the right will eat player input and block aiming. Even on desktop, avoid it so the layout stays consistent across devices.
- **Bottom corners on mobile**: camera button, action buttons, emote button, joystick. Call `J.setUISettings({ mobileCameraButton: false, ... })` to hide them only if you fully own that area.

Safe zones, in order of preference:
- **Left middle vertical** (e.g. `position: fixed; top: 50%; left: 16px; transform: translateY(-50%);`). Empty in every mode. Best for status readouts like score, currency, win streak. This is what DrawPerfect uses for its money and streak HUD.
- **Top center horizontal** (e.g. `position: fixed; top: 16px; left: 50%; transform: translateX(-50%);`). Empty. Good for round timers, mission objectives, banners.
- **Bottom center** (e.g. `position: fixed; bottom: 40px; left: 50%; transform: translateX(-50%);`). Safe on desktop, conflicts with mobile controls unless you have hidden them.

Test every HUD in fullscreen at 1024x576 (a common Chromebook resolution) to confirm the safe zones do not collapse at small sizes. If a layout works at 1920x1080 but covers the engine controls at 1024x576, that is a placement bug, not a styling one.

## The four rules

### Rule 1: One `<style>` tag, all visuals as classes

Inject a single id keyed style tag at startup. All colors, fonts, layout, and animations live there. No inline styles for static visuals.

```ts
const STYLE_ID = "myhud-style";

function injectStyles(): void {
  let style = document.getElementById(STYLE_ID) as HTMLStyleElement | null;
  if (!style) {
    style = document.createElement("style");
    style.id = STYLE_ID;
    document.head.appendChild(style);
  }
  style.textContent = `
    #myhud-score {
      position: fixed; top: 16px; left: 16px;
      color: white;
      font: bold 24px Quicksand, sans-serif;
      text-shadow: 0 2px 4px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.9);
    }
    #myhud-score.low { color: #ff2222; }
  `;
}
```

Why: CSS is parsed once and cached. Class toggles are GPU cheap. The browser's style engine handles cascade, no JS work per frame. The `id` on the `<style>` tag makes the injection idempotent so hot reload does not duplicate it.

### Rule 2: Build the DOM once, store persistent refs

Every element created at startup via `document.createElement`. References stored in module vars or a class field. Never recreated.

```ts
let scoreEl: HTMLDivElement | undefined;

function buildUI(host: HTMLElement) {
  injectStyles();
  scoreEl = document.createElement("div");
  scoreEl.id = "myhud-score";
  host.appendChild(scoreEl);
}

J.onGameStart(() => {
  if (J.uiElement) buildUI(J.uiElement);
});
```

Hot reload safe: check `document.getElementById(...)` first and rebind to existing nodes if they survived the reload.

For lists (leaderboard rows, top players, score table): pre build N row containers at startup, each with a stable shape (rank, name, score spans). Hide unused rows with a `.hidden` class. Do not rebuild the list as an HTML string.

### Rule 3: `textContent` updates, gated by last value cache

Track the last written value per cell. Skip the DOM write when nothing changed.

```ts
let lastScore = -1;

J.onGameTick(() => {
  if (!scoreEl) return;
  const score = getScore();
  if (score !== lastScore) {
    scoreEl.textContent = `Score: ${score}`;
    lastScore = score;
  }
});
```

Always prefer `textContent` over `innerHTML`. `textContent` skips the HTML parser and the subtree teardown, and it cannot inject markup. Use `innerHTML` only when the text legitimately needs HTML (a death message with a `<br>`, a notification with a colored `<span>`), and only on event handlers, never per tick.

For per row data in a list, store last values per row:

```ts
type LbRow = {
  el: HTMLDivElement;
  nameEl: HTMLDivElement;
  scoreEl: HTMLDivElement;
  lastName: string;
  lastScore: number;
};

function updateRow(row: LbRow, name: string, score: number) {
  if (row.lastName !== name) {
    row.nameEl.textContent = name;
    row.lastName = name;
  }
  if (row.lastScore !== score) {
    row.scoreEl.textContent = String(score);
    row.lastScore = score;
  }
}
```

### Rule 4: Animations live in CSS, JS just toggles classes

Define wobble, pulse, fade as `@keyframes`. Trigger by adding a class. To replay an animation, remove the class, force a reflow, then re add.

```css
.myhud-arrow.wobble { animation: myhud-elastic 0.4s ease-out; }
@keyframes myhud-elastic {
  0%   { transform: scale(1); }
  16%  { transform: scale(2.4); }
  44%  { transform: scale(1.3); }
  100% { transform: scale(1); }
}
```

```ts
function replayWobble(el: HTMLElement) {
  el.classList.remove("wobble");
  void el.offsetWidth;  // force reflow so the next add starts fresh
  el.classList.add("wobble");
}
```

For elements that animate in, sit for a bit, fade out, then remove themselves (kill feed entries, toast notifications), chain two animations and listen for `animationend` on the fade:

```css
.kf-entry {
  animation: myhud-elastic 0.4s ease-out, kf-fade 1s 4s forwards;
}
@keyframes kf-fade {
  from { opacity: 1; }
  to   { opacity: 0; }
}
```

```ts
function pushEntry(container: HTMLElement, text: string) {
  const el = document.createElement("div");
  el.className = "kf-entry";
  el.textContent = text;
  el.addEventListener("animationend", (e) => {
    if ((e as AnimationEvent).animationName === "kf-fade") el.remove();
  });
  container.appendChild(el);
}
```

After the entry is created, JS never touches it again. The compositor handles the whole lifecycle.

## Worked example: score widget with a bump on change

A self contained widget that uses all four rules together. Drop it into a fresh project and it works.

```ts
import * as J from "jamango";

const STYLE_ID = "score-hud-style";
let scoreEl: HTMLDivElement | undefined;
let lastScore = -1;

function injectStyles(): void {
  let style = document.getElementById(STYLE_ID) as HTMLStyleElement | null;
  if (!style) {
    style = document.createElement("style");
    style.id = STYLE_ID;
    document.head.appendChild(style);
  }
  style.textContent = `
    #score-hud {
      position: fixed; top: 16px; left: 16px;
      color: white;
      font: bold 24px Quicksand, sans-serif;
      text-shadow: 0 2px 4px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.9);
    }
    #score-hud.bump { animation: score-pop 0.3s ease-out; }
    @keyframes score-pop {
      0%   { transform: scale(1); }
      40%  { transform: scale(1.4); }
      100% { transform: scale(1); }
    }
  `;
}

function buildUI(host: HTMLElement): void {
  injectStyles();
  scoreEl = document.createElement("div");
  scoreEl.id = "score-hud";
  scoreEl.textContent = "0";
  host.appendChild(scoreEl);
}

function setScore(value: number): void {
  if (!scoreEl || value === lastScore) return;
  scoreEl.textContent = String(value);
  scoreEl.classList.remove("bump");
  void scoreEl.offsetWidth;  // force reflow so the next add replays the animation
  scoreEl.classList.add("bump");
  lastScore = value;
}

J.onGameStart(() => {
  if (J.uiElement) buildUI(J.uiElement);
});

J.onGameTick(() => {
  // Replace getCurrentScore() with your own state source (a trait, a module var, etc.)
  setScore(getCurrentScore());
});
```

Trace it through the four rules:
1. **One `<style>` tag.** `injectStyles` writes the rule and the keyframe once. Hot reload safe via the `id`.
2. **Build the DOM once.** `buildUI` creates `scoreEl` in `onGameStart` and stores the ref. Never recreated.
3. **`textContent` + last value cache.** `setScore` short circuits when the value did not change, so the steady state tick touches the DOM zero times.
4. **CSS animation, JS toggles class.** The bump runs on the compositor, JS does no scale math per frame. The `remove` + reflow + `add` dance replays the animation on each new score.

## Anti patterns

These show up in old HUD code and tank Chromebook frame rate. Search for them.

| Anti pattern | Cost | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `filter: drop-shadow(...)` | Per pixel GPU work, very expensive on weak GPUs | `text-shadow` for text. Drop entirely for icons. |
| `will-change: transform, opacity` on many elements | Each becomes its own compositor layer, eats VRAM | Remove. The browser auto promotes elements that actually animate. |
| `backface-visibility: hidden` for "GPU acceleration" | Same layer promotion problem, usually cargo cult | Remove. |
| `-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased` everywhere | No benefit and muddies inspection | Remove. |
| `element.innerHTML = template` in `onGameTick` | HTML parse + DOM teardown + layout every frame | Persistent elements + `textContent` per cell |
| Computing `transform: scale(${x})` per frame in JS | Per tick string allocation and style write | CSS `@keyframes` + class toggle |
| Per tick style writes without diffs | Forces style recalculation even for no ops | Cache last value, gate the write |
| Listening to `keydown` on `document` from module scope | Adds a new listener every script recompile (leak) | Use `J.onControlPress` / `J.onControlRelease` |

 
## Quick checklist

Before shipping a HUD or always visible UI:

- [ ] All visuals in one `<style>` tag with classes (no per element inline styles for static visuals)
- [ ] All elements created once at startup, refs stored in module vars
- [ ] Per tick writes use `textContent` (or `innerHTML` only for HTML y text on event, never per tick)
- [ ] Every per tick write gated by `if (last !== value) return`
- [ ] All animations as `@keyframes`, triggered by class toggles
- [ ] No `filter: drop-shadow`, no `will-change`, no `backface-visibility`
- [ ] Lists use stable per row elements, not rebuilt HTML strings
- [ ] Transient animated elements clean themselves up via `animationend`
- [ ] Keyboard input via `J.onControlPress` / `J.onControlRelease`, not raw `document` events
